nodes.title: Add "changes requested" & Statuses+Checks conclusion to `pr list`

nodes.body:
New functionality:
- resolve conclusion between multiple Statuses/Checks into a single status
- print only failed statuses next to PRs - assumes that "success" and "pending" statuses are not actionable information
- print failed statuses in red
- also apply "changes requested" and Statuses information next to PR for current branch
- tweak output alignment, widths; truncate long titles

Deliberately does _not_ show "changes requested" nor Statuses/Checks for PRs that request your review:
- PRs that request your review might already have reviews from other people, and one of them might be "changes requested". This information is likely not relevant to the fact that you should also leave a review.
- These PRs might also have failing statuses. For now, I've also chosen not to display them, since these PRs are from other people who asked your review, and it's likely that those other people are responsible for fixing failing checks.

Will merge this soon since I want to use this in a demo ✨

This looks great! I agree with everything but have some doubts about the "print only failed statuses" part. Since pending and success will be indistinguishable, I'm thinking that pending is actionable because otherwise I'd just assume my PR changes passed CI. Even though the action is "I guess I'll wait until it finishes" I'd still want to know that bit of information.

nodes.body:
💫 Now ready for review! ✨ 

```go
// Context represents the interface for querying information about the current environment
type Context interface {
	AuthToken() (string, error)
	AuthLogin() (string, error)
	Branch() (string, error)
	SetBranch(string)
	Remotes() (Remotes, error)
	BaseRepo() (*GitHubRepository, error)
	SetBaseRepo(string)
}

// example usage:
branch, err := context.Current().Branch()
```

Design goals:
- lazy loading, i.e. individual data is only fetched when requested
- caching, i.e. data is never re-requested from the filesystem
- unit-tested code
- command tests run with their own isolated context
- ability to override parts of context via `--repo, -R` and `--current-branch, -B` global cli flags
- setting `GH_REPO=foo/bar` is equivalent to `gh --repo foo/bar`
- :fire: the legacy `github` package!

@vilmibm To reach the above goals, I had to restructure and rename a lot of the existing context comands. Your code served as a great outline for me, but in the end I mostly rewrote the whole branch. I hope you don't mind! Please let me know what you think 🙇 

Breaking:
- If there isn't a well-formed `~/.config/hub` file, the program will panic. We will refine authentication and the initialization of a fresh config file with #6.

Update:
- keep original order of git remotes (applies when using `remotes.FindByName('*')`)
- avoid crash on malformed yaml config file
- restructure SSH config parser (used when translating `git@...` or `ssh://` remote URLs), avoid ever reading system or user `~/.ssh/config` files in tests
- more tests!

> * Is there an advantage to using `context.Current()` over automatically creating  currentContext in the package's `Init` function?

That's a great question. Right now it doesn't seem like there is a clear advantage, but when designing this, I was trying to be a bit defensive about putting all context methods as being "global" in the `context.*` scope, and instead made the context an instance that you can obtain a reference to (and swap it around during tests). I've done so that commands could _theoretically_ hold on to their own context instance in the future and that could potentially enable running tests in parallel. If we ran them in parallel now, they would experience race conditions in which one test initializing a mock context could affect the run of another test.

But as things are wired up right now, there's not a clear benefit from calling `context.Current().Branch()` vs. `context.Branch()`, except perhaps revealing that there is an underlying context instance which is stateful.

> `BaseRepo` / `AuthLogin`

This is good feedback around naming. I'll try to rename them according to your suggestions so that their intent is more immediately apparent.

nodes.title: Stop requiring vendored dependencies in CI

nodes.body: i am very supportive of this! possibly a dumb question, but why wouldn't this PR also remove the `vendor/` directory?

> but why wouldn't this PR also remove the `vendor/` directory?

Good question! Because we've checked into git so many different files that we ultimately don't want to keep (`vendor/*`, hub stuff, a 10MB `gh-cli` binary by accident) I wanted to propose that we get rid of unwanted git object cruft by rewriting history and using git-filter-branch to scrub out all unwanted objects. This would considerably slim down every future `git clone` of this repository. If this sounds like a worthwhile endeavor for you (and you're ready to be inconvenienced by having to reset your local git branch one time to a force-pushed upstream), then we would do it as a follow-up and most likely between dev cycles (once all outstanding PRs have been merged).

> Are we using Octofactory in our actions that build the app?

Not explicitly, but we automatically benefit from it. It also enables us to potentially use any private `github/*` modules (but we don't have a need for any yet).

> I wanted to propose that we get rid of unwanted git object cruft by rewriting history and using git-filter-branch to scrub out all unwanted object.

YES! Let's do it!
I'm fine with this! Seems better to do it now than later and I don't mind force resetting.

```
I've hit a wall with this for today. Locally I can get it working with `goreleaser --snapshot --skip-publish --rm-dist`. But when it is run as an action this is the error I get:

> ##[error]   ⨯ release failed after 0.12s error=failed to build for linux_amd64: main.go:7:2: cannot > find package "github.com/github/gh-cli/command" in any of:
> 	/opt/hostedtoolcache/go/1.10.8/x64/src/github.com/github/gh-cli/command (from $GOROOT)
>	/home/runner/go/src/github.com/github/gh-cli/command (from $GOPATH)
>
> ##[error]The process '/home/runner/work/_temp/0d09158f-9529-4bd1-a5fc-2b2ec20c3b77/goreleaser' failed with exit code 1
> ##[error]Node run failed with exit code 1


My guess is that this has something to do with go modules, but I can't figure it out yet.
```
Turns out it was go module related. I needed to make goreleaser use go v1.13. The action seems to work now, but I have no idea where the release it put!

I'm still working on this PR. 

> (in case we don't manage to figure out how it works; in my mind it looks slightly over-engineered for our needs)

goreleaser is over-engineered for what we need, but it also leaves a pretty tiny footprint on our project (a github action and a .goreleaser.yml file). I'm ok with using it for now because it does a lot of stuff automatically for us and it will be easy to swap out if we decide not to use it in the future.

All that being said, I want to though goreleaser in off a bridge because it works perfectly except I can't get it to automatically update the brew formula. I'm still trying to figure out why that is, hopefully I will solve it in my dreams tonight.
@probablycorey You can use this to update the Homebrew formula, by yours truly https://github.com/marketplace/actions/bump-homebrew-formula 💖

Automating our build process isn’t too difficult. But it turns out automating the install process IS difficult! Here are the options we have and why each of them stink in their own special way.

- Host the gh binary as a release on a private repo
	- using brew or curl will fail because they don’t have the credentials to authenticate with GitHub. This means people would have to enter they username/password/2fa and then use duo to download the binary. 
- Use a tap and have  `brew`  build the repo (this is what https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh does)
	- You need to have git setup to use credentials, most developers will already have this.
	- You need to have the entire go build setup. This is annoying because it will take time, but brew handles this.
	- We don’t have as much control over the release unless we update the formula after each build.
	- It will only work for GitHubbers
- Host the gh binary as a release on a public repo
	- This works, but anyone will be able to see the binary and download it. 
	- If we want this automated, we’ll need to write a GitHub action to move the binary over to the public repo
- Host the gh binary as a release on a private repo (but don’t use an install script)
	- This will work for GitHubbers, but it is annoying for them because they need to go to a website, download a tarball, untar it, and then install it in usr/local/bin. 


Based on these I'm going to start working on the `Host the gh binary as a release on a public repo` option. I think this makes the most sense because it will work for githubbers and non-githubbers, and it is a pretty simple solution. I just need a github action to copy the release to another repo.

@mislav I think this is finally ready to look at with you. If we like this, then we need to do three things.

1. Pick a public repo to host the binaries and homebrew formula (it is using a temp repo I created called probablycorey/homebrew-gghh right now)
2. Add a PAT to access the public repo
3. Add your https://github.com/mislav/bump-homebrew-formula-action action on the public repo so the formula gets bumped.

Since this is installing a binary asset, I wasn't sure how to make your bump-homebrew-formula-action setup work, so I want to check in with you about that.

This works, I tested it out by creating this tag https://github.com/github/gh-cli/releases/tag/v0.0.114 and a release was created, binaries built, binaries copied to https://github.com/probablycorey/homebrew-gghh/releases, and the brew formula was updated.

Some things I did after talking with @mislav
-[x] Instead of grabbing the latest release, I use the `GITHUB_REF` env var to grab the tag ref
-[ ] @mislav is concerned about race conditions around asset creation. But I looked into the gorelease code and what I saw was it synchronously builds and uploads the assets before the `copy-release` action is called. But there is a possibility I don't fully understand the race condition.
-[ ] Use inputs/outputs on actions to pass the download url to the "bump brew" action.
-[ ] Switched to typescript and https://github.com/zeit/ncc based on @mislavs suggestion. I don't love how I have to remember to run `npm run build` after every change, but I do love the type checking that typescript gives me.

The releases are still going to probablycorey/homebrew-gghh. I'm fine with moving to a more official repo now, or we can do that in another PR. I think reusing the `github/homebrew-gh` repo and making it public makes sense.

Re: race conditions: the only way to create a Release with assets is to first create a Release (1 API call) and then attach assets to it one by one (N API calls). This process can be [seen in goreleaser here](https://github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser/blob/93259b068367f43289705a42b15f275864f66dbb/internal/pipe/release/release.go#L121-L147).

So if you have an Action that is triggered on `v*` tag push or the "release create" event, that one executes as soon as the Release was created (first API call), but there's no guarantee that assets have finished uploading yet.

1. Release created 👈 "release create" (and `v*` tag push) actions run triggered here
2. Asset 1 finished uploading
2. Asset 2 finished uploading
2. …

This problem is not very likely to bite us since assets typically upload _faster_ than Actions spins up a job. I just wanted us to be aware of this in case Actions runs start being more efficient and we see some oddities down the line.


> I think reusing the `github/homebrew-gh` repo and making it public makes sense.

That makes sense for me too!

Thanks for all the hard work on this 👍
It works, it's on `github/homebrew-gh` and I am very very happy. Please review my code because I can't look at it with any objectivity anymore!

I've taken this and ran with it.

- switched to `getInput` instead of reading `INPUT_*`
- have the action work if target Release already exists
- now both "macOS" and "linux" packages are copied

Two questions:
- will `gh -v` expected to also work?
- should a note be made in the `gh - help` page about how to check the version?

Idea: add in a link to the release notes like `gh --version` => "gh version VERSION (DATE) (LINK)"

> * will `gh -v` expected to also work?

It doesn't work right now, and maybe we could make it work (not sure if our CLI framework enables that), but I don't feel like it's strictly needed. Because sometimes `-v` means "verbose" or another flag, commands usually settle for long-form `--version` or `version` to display a version. Git, for instance, doesn't support `-v`.

> * should a note be made in the `gh - help` page about how to check the version?

That's a good point! Our CLI framework ensures that it's already there:

```
Usage:
  gh [command]

Available Commands:
  help        Help about any command
  pr          Work with pull requests

Flags:
  -B, --current-branch string   current git branch
  -h, --help                    help for gh
  -R, --repo string             current GitHub repository
      --version                 version for gh
```

I think it's best to still show usage when an unknown command is provided; that's pretty standard behavior.

I don't mind the verbosity of adding `cmd.SilenceUsage = true` to commands as a trade off, personally.

nodes.body: @mislav I feel like the spirit of this issue has largely been accomplished, does closing it make sense?

Sure, good call!

There is definitely follow-up items I want to do around this, such as rename the `context` package to something different to reduce ambiguity with Go stdlib "context" package, which we might also be using in the future.

> The app currently determines the context it's operating under from several sources:
> 
> * the filesystem (e.g. current directory name)
> * app config file (currently `~/.config/hub` in YAML format)
> * environment variables (e.g. `GH_REPO`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, etc.)
> * git config (via `git config` & `git remote -v`, sources: `.git/config` & `~/.gitconfig`)
> * git tree (e.g. current branch, current commit SHA)
> * SSH config (sources: `~/.ssh/config` & `/etc/ssh_config`) - resolves SSH aliases for hostnames listed under git remotes
> * GitHub API (e.g. info about the current repo)
> 
> When something like `gh pr create` gets run, quite a lot happens under the hood. For example:
> 
> 1. Current GitHub user and OAuth token are obtained from app config;
> 2. The list of git remotes gets queried and parsed;
> 3. The "main" remote (i.e. one pointing to the canonical GitHub repo) is determined by searching for the first one in this list: `upstream`, `github`, `origin`;
> 4. The base branch is determined via `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/<REMOTE>/HEAD` (alternatively, by querying repo information via API);
> 5. The head branch is determined by looking at the push target for the current branch:
>    
>    * explicit upstream branch configuration is first looked up;
>    * otherwise, the first remote that has a same-named tracking branch is the likely push target;
>    * otherwise, assume the branch isn't pushed yet, so determine the first remote that points to a GitHub project that the current user has write capabilities to;
>    * if such a remote doesn't exist, create one by forking;
> 6. A person's preferred text editor is looked up for authoring PR description text;
> 7. PR creation operation proceeds.
> 
> To facilitate all these lookups, the current codebase has a loose system of mapping one piece of information to another. For example:
> 
> * current git repo 👉 default ("base") branch
> * current git repo 👉 "main" remote
> * git remote 👉 GitHub repository (a.k.a. "project") it maps to
> * tracking branch 👉 git remote it belongs to
> * current user 👉 the person's fork
> 
> Since a lot of lookups start from the current git repo (based on the current working directory at the time that the CLI app runs), there is a LocalRepo struct that encapsulates performing some of these mappings, while additional mapping logic is scattered across individual methods such as `Branch.RemoteName()`, `Remote.Project()`, etc. Some problems I find with the current system:
> 
> * Inconsistent naming (e.g. "repository" vs. "project", the ambiguity of "branch")
> * Blurred responsibility between objects (e.g. why would a Branch have to know how to map itself to a Remote, or a Remote to a Project?)
> * Methods that do too much but don't sufficiently betray intent (e.g. `LocalRepo.RemoteBranchAndProject()`)
> * Hard to stub out in tests (ideally, unit tests should be able to set up a mock context in memory rather than having to write a test git repo out to fileystem)
> * **All** of this code is under the same Go package: "github".
> 
> My rough proposal for starting to address this:
> 
> * [x]  Get rid of methods and structs named "Project" from the codebase;[x]  Get rid of LocalRepo;[x]  Design an abstraction around `git config` that is able to mock responses in memory;[x]  Consider moving `github/branch.go` to under the "git" package;[ ]  Consider isolating `git/ssh_config.go` to its own package;[ ]  Minimize the implementation of Remote and instead perform necessary mappings through a separate service object;[x]  Minimize the "github" package until it's ideally gone;[ ]  Write Go unit tests along the way to confirm the testability of these implementations.
> 
> Let's revise all this as we go along! Thank you for reading. 🙇

nodes.body: Re: "Reimagine how the app determines its context": my intent was not to suggest that all the goals I've outlined in #2 are reachable within the course of a week. Rather, I had imagined this as something to keep in mind _over the long term_ as we develop prototype and stable features and perform mini-refactoring of underlying mechanisms as we go along.
Finalized my work on `gh pr list`, `gh pr checkout` for this week, demo here: https://github.com/github/ce-cli/issues/14

Here is a summary of my previous week and some thoughts about what we should do next week:

**Last week:**
- Focused on moving the good parts of prototyping over to master
- Created a Graphql client that we can use to query the GitHub api
- Started on some test infrastructure to test the `gh pr list` command
- Worked on making the dev experience for `gh` less tedius https://github.com/github/gh-cli/pull/15

**Next week**
- We agree on a general error handling pattern (don't use panic and bubble errors up to the top. But we still don't have a pattern for presenting the errors to users. I want us to implement that pattern this week.
- The output formatting (color, style) of the PR commands didn't get done last week. Since @mislav and I are on `team master` I want to talk with him about the approach I used in the prototype and to come up with what we want to bring over to master.
- Implement `gh pr show`
- Implement `gh pr create`
- Implement `gh help`
- Move @mislav's auth stuff over to master
- Get @vilmibm's `context` work in master
Next issue https://github.com/github/gh-cli/issues/16
https://github.com/cli/cli/issues/4

nodes.body:
Today I wrestled with `gh pr checkout` and I've got some questions for @mislav and @vilmibm. There are a few options:

1. We can use code similar to hub and the prototype https://github.com/github/gh-cli/blob/prototype/command/pr.go#L408-L456. 
2. We can use code similar to what github/desktop uses https://github.com/desktop/desktop/blob/3db6fc736e4e91827eae9db55b88235941cebec0/app/src/lib/stores/app-store.ts#L5141 (I think it fetches the remote if it doesn't exist and then checks it out)
3. We can use git pull references like in this examples https://github.community/t5/How-to-use-Git-and-GitHub/Checkout-a-branch-from-a-fork/m-p/78/highlight/true#M11

Choice 1 works, but I am having trouble figuring out what each step does and why. But maybe pairing with @mislav could help me understand
Choice 2's pattern seems easier to understand, but maybe it's missing some edge cases?
Choice 3 is the very simple, but I feel like I must be something about Git and GitHub which makes this solution impractical.

Another choice I have to make is, when you checkout the branch should we use the branch name `org-name:branch-name` or should we use the pr number `pr/4`. Desktop use the `pr/4` pattern, my initial leaning is towards the `org-name:branch-name` direction, but using the `pr/number` pattern gives all PR branches a nice clean namespace.

If we didn't want to allow for easy `git push` back to the PR, the `pr checkout` command would be trivial to implement. It would literally come down to this:

```sh
git fetch origin refs/pull/NUMBER/head
git checkout -b newbranch FETCH_HEAD
```

This works, but in this new branch:
1. `git pull` without arguments won't do the Right Thing™
2. `git push` without arguments won't do the Right Thing.

Solving (1) is not that hard, but for solving (2) with default git behavior (`git config push.default` == "simple"), you would have to ensure that the checked out branch has the same name as the head branch, and that correct tracking configuration is set up. See https://github.com/github/hub/issues/1495#issuecomment-310065377 for background.

We do currently handle all these cases in the `prototype` branch. Since the code I've ported from hub was likely confusing, and because I won't have time to pair with you today, I've sprinkled comments to clarify things: https://github.com/github/gh-cli/commit/981792490b173683bd60ca0edf41aac1eacf7c7b. Hope it helps!
Thanks @mislav!

~I'm calling the pr create improvements for this week "done" without a merge; given all the changes in master i'd rather cleanly port pr create over next week and then do the reset of `prototype` to `master`.~

For some reason I thought my PR was essentially un-mergable but it was fine, so i've marked it ready for review

I decided to punt on `gh status` after talking to @billygriffin about it.

I think the original idea behind `gh status` was something that could wrap `git status` and also tell you about things in the world of Pull Requests you might care about. I couldn't think of a way to make `gh status` useful given the functionality that's ended up in `gh pr...` and also didn't want to do any `git` wrapping.

Until we have an idea of how to make `gh status` useful without wrapping `git` or being redundant with `gh pr...` I'd rather see us work on other stuff.
demo: https://github.com/github/ce-cli/issues/15

`gh pr checkout` didn't make it into master this week :( This is where I got https://github.com/github/gh-cli/compare/pr-checkout?expand=1 in case people want to draw inspiration from it next week. My goal was to understand what was happening in this code in more detail https://github.com/github/hub/issues/1495#issuecomment-310065377. Like @mislav said above, getting it to work with pull is not bad, but getting push to work as well is tricky and more work because everything I read said you need to make sure the local branch name is exactly the same as the remote branch name. 

One thing that might make things easier is if we add remotes if the PR is a fork. But I couldn't get a good understanding of the downsides to this idea.

nodes.body: cc @billygriffin @nerdneha @ampinsk @vilmibm @mislav @tierninho

This looks great! Makes perfect sense to me. Re: 

> I've also had a backlog column where we put things we want to put into the next sprint. I've defaulted to simplicity and left this out for now.

If I was being picky, this might be the one thing I'd ask for, but +1 on starting simple and seeing if we need it first

This looks good but, like @ampinsk, I would also prefer that we split the "To Do" column into two columns: “Icebox” and “Prioritized”. I don't think we're getting too much ahead of ourselves by doing that?

Thank you for putting this together!

In the past I've preferred a split TODO and backlog but the lines between the two often became blurred or unclear after a while. I'm up for trying a single TODO for now and seeing how that goes.

The other column that has gotten weird over time is Done; do we have clear consumers of this column? If not, should we just be archiving the cards that would end up here? If people are checking that column, at what cadence should it be cleared out? weekly?

+1 on thanks for putting this together @probablycorey!

A few thoughts and one question, and I don't feel strongly about any of this. 

Generally I'm not a huge fan of the idea of an "icebox" because stuff tends to just languish there, and if it's in the icebox maybe there's a case that it shouldn't be on the board. But I think that's a slightly separate question from `Backlog`, which encompasses all things that we actually intend to do for sure, and `To do` or `This week`, which is just the most current ones. I have no strong preference because as long as the top things in backlog are actually the most important, than it serves roughly the same purpose, just doesn't provide a sense of timeboxing in a "sprint"-like period.

The other question I wanted to ask is whether both issues & PRs go in the board. We've struggled with that on Desktop because they tend to get separated and there's no way to couple them, so issues kind of hang out weirdly until the PR is actually merged. I don't have any great ideas for how to mitigate this, just wanted to clarify how y'all wanted to handle that.

> The other column that has gotten weird over time is Done

Done is weird, but with project automation it ends up moving things there automatically and then you just have to remember to clear it after a sprint is complete.

>  I wanted to ask is whether both issues & PRs 

Yeah, this annoys me too. I usually have issues in "to do, in progress" and PRs go in "in progress, needs review, needs to be merged". So I also don't have a way around the "hang out weirdly" problem.

As for everything else. The `backlog` column was added and filled so the project board working. So I'm going to close this out.

nodes.title: Take templates into account for `pr create` and `issue create`

nodes.body: i love it!
Took a stab at this in the [flags section at the bottom of the design spec ](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1bkKuJN6tFrfpAsA_yuThyVVtT79CvoM5solKSrxlMRs/edit#heading=h.7nm8gqwotnan)

Based on the current design spec, enumerating some engineering tasks:

- [x] Use sentence case for everything, including flag help text
- [x] Drop `-B, --current-branch` global flag
- [x] `pr`: change shorthand for `--base` to `-B`
- [ ] `pr create`: support `--web`
- [x] `pr list`: support `-a, --assignee`
- [x] ~~Explore if we can replace Cobra's `string`, `int`, `strings`, `stringArray` etc. with something more human-friendly~~ <ins>I've looked into this and this would be too much hassle to do before the alpha. These values don't come from Cobra, but from the `pflag` package that Cobra is using, and this extra level of indirection makes it harder to change.</ins>
Updated the issue with what was in the doc! This should be ready to go whenever we want to prioritize it.

We already build a Windows binary as part of our release process, but the process of extracting the zip archive and adding it to one's PATH in Windows can be tedious, so to improve the installation exerience I would suggest one or more of the following approaches (none mutually exclusive):

* A Windows installer experience (e.g. git & Heroku CLI installers) https://github.com/github/ce-cli/issues/6#issuecomment-533124383

nodes.title: Notify users when update available and allow them to update

nodes.body:
Before I get too deep into this I wanted to validate some thinking. Here are some assumptions that might not be correct.

1. To avoid adding extra time to each execution, we will only check for a new release after X hours have passed.
2. We don't want to remind the user after each execution that there is a new version available, show the message once every X hours
3. Assumption 1 & 2 requires that we keep state somewhere. In order to reuse code I'm assuming we should reuse gh's config file.

Based on that the logic of the "check for upgrade" flow would look like this...

```
There are three flags, UPGRADE_CHECKED_AT, UPGRADE_VERSION, and UPGRADE_REMINDED_AT

- if not tty, exit
- Get the current version
- if current version == UPGRADE_VERSION exit
- if UPGRADE_REMINDED_AT is set and is less than 24 hours old, exit
- if UPGRADE_REMINDED_AT is set and more than 24 hours old, ping the user, update UPGRADE_REMINDED_AT and exit

- if UPGRADE_CHECKED_AT < 24 hours exit
- Get the released version
- update UPGRADE_CHECKED_AT and UPGRADE_VERSION
- If the current version equals the released version exit
```

@billygriffin @vilmibm @mislav should I change any of my assumptions? Am I overcomplicating this?

> To avoid adding extra time to each execution, we will only check for a new release after X hours have passed.

> We don't want to remind the user after each execution that there is a new version available, show the message once every X hours

Both of these seem completely reasonable to me from a user perspective! I would definitely not want it to seem like we're spamming people with update notifications if they're just trying to use the tool to get their work done and don't want to update right now.

An example of how npm does this

![](https://d.pr/i/r4RFV5+)

Most of your laid out plan I agree with, @probablycorey.

> 3\. Assumption 1 & 2 requires that we keep state somewhere. In order to reuse code I'm assuming we should reuse gh's config file.

We can do that for now, but ideally I would like us to save state elsewhere than our main config file. When gh's config file includes more useful configuration, and after we figure out how to securely save an OAuth token elsewhere than to this file, `~/.config/gh` will theoretically become suitable and safe to check into version control by people who version their dotfiles, but if we keep updating it with a timestamp every 24h, then we break that use-case.

Closing since we've shipped a release that checks for and notifies about version upgrades! 

The remaining work is around improving that mechanism (e.g. avoid checking too often, give concrete upgrade instructions such as “brew upgrade gh” when applicable, etc.), but these are going to be performed as follow-up tasks.

nodes.body:
I'm going to split this into three stages.
- [ ] An automated way to catalog potential release notes from PR titles (Like desktop does in https://github.com/desktop/desktop/blob/development/script/draft-release/run.ts)
- [ ] Use the result of step 1 to build release notes for github/homebrew-gh
- [ ] Add the `gh --changelog` flag that will show the release notes.
Any value or reason to make `gh changelog` an alternative cmd, like we did with `gh version`?
Based on what I've learned, I think using actions to do this is probably more complicated than it needs to be. See https://github.com/github/gh-cli/pull/189 for more details.

This is trickier than I thought. Here are some options and I'm not sure what the best way forward is.

# Use a `changelog.txt` file

This is easy to edit, easy edit for people to find in the repo, and easy for scripts to manipulate. But because we compile to a binary it is hard to get that text into the app via `gh changelog`. I think the way to do this is to add an extra build step to our makefile and to .goreleaser.yml that will convert the changelog into a go file.

# Use a `changelog.json` file

This is a lot like the `changelog.txt` but it makes writing scripts to update it a little easier.

# Use a `changelog.go` file

This makes it very easy to display the changelog in `gh changelog`, but it is less readable by users, and it's harder to write scripts that will directly edit a go file. It is also harder to turn this go file into something we can post in the relase notes on https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh/releases/tag/v0.3.5.

----

I feel like I've spent too much time thinking about this and there is a very simple solution that eludes me. But I can't figure it out.

Perhaps the easiest solution for now is:

`gh changelog` = opens browser tab to the new marketing site > release notes page

As for generating the changelog to use in online release notes, I've spiked something really quick: #199

Now, assuming that we've gotten the GitHub Releases changelog out of the way, the next step is "linking" those notes into the binary so that `gh changelog` can do something useful. Personally, **I don't feel really strongly that we should have a command to display the changelog; perhaps we could simply link to release notes from the man page** (`gh help`). If we really do want to have a special command for this, I second @tierninho's excellent idea that we could simply have it open a URL, rather than having to figure out how to embed release notes into the binary itself. That way, the definite source for a changelog is the GitHub Release itself (which can also be amended by hand post-release if we want to polish up the notes).
i agree with mislav's assessment re: not needing a changelog command. we have `gh version` which could include a link to a release with a changelog and also link from `gh help`.

> Personally, I don't feel really strongly that we should have a command to display the changelog; perhaps we could simply link to release notes from the man page (gh help)

I can get behind that too. Looking at `gh help` it is a little crowded. Having the release notes link with the version command makes sense to me since release notes and version are pretty linked. How do people feel about havint `gh version` output something like...

```
gh version 0.3.5 (2020-01-06)
Release Notes: https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh/releases/tag/v0.3.5
```

> How do people feel about havint gh version output something like...

👍 on the idea and in favor of dropping `gh-changelog` altogether we implement this

nodes.title: Cannot download and install CLI on Mac

nodes.body: Site note: We should probably add the remove/uninstall cmd for MacOS to the ReadMe: https://github.com/github/gh-cli#macos

@tierninho Hmm, it seems like the `github/gh` Homebrew tap isn't auto-updating for you. The `v6.6.6` release was an accident and we've [reverted it last week](https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh/commit/cf95c0c0d24ee41394b0044705e46d1e58216d3d). I rememeber you've had this trouble before, too: https://github.com/github/ce-cli/issues/10#issuecomment-539947533. Do you have any `HOMEBREW_*` environment variables set?

This should work in any case:
```sh
brew uninstall -f gh
brew update # typically not needed, Homebrew automatically runs this in 5-min intervals
brew install github/gh/gh
```

Note that even if you de-authorized "GitHub CLI" in your GitHub Settings, its oauth token is still stored in `~/.config/gh`. CLI will attempt to use this (now invalid) token for API requests and fail. _We don't yet handle this case_, so you will have to manually remove `~/.config/gh` too in order to re-authenticate. I will make a note for CLI to handle this better in the future.

```sh
# force re-auth
rm -f ~/.config/gh
```

Agreed re: remove/uninstall instructions!

Thanks @mislav but still blocked. I tried without `brew update` but that ran into the download error above. With `brew update` is here:

```
➜  desktop git:(development) brew uninstall -f gh

➜  desktop git:(development) brew update
git@github.com: Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.

Please make sure you have the correct access rights
and the repository exists.
Error: Fetching /usr/local/Homebrew/Library/Taps/github/homebrew-gh failed!
➜  desktop git:(development) ~/.gitconfig
zsh: permission denied: /Users/tierninho/.gitconfig
➜  desktop git:(development)
```

Let me know if you need to see output of `brew doctor` or `brew config`.

> Do you have any HOMEBREW_* environment variables set?

I do not think so.

It looks like Homebrew couldn't update the `github/homebrew-gh` tap due to not being able to fetch over SSH.

Do you normally use SSH git URLs with GitHub? If not, you can switch to HTTPS:

```sh
git --git-dir=/usr/local/Homebrew/Library/Taps/github/homebrew-gh/.git remote set-url https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh.git
```

HTTPS should have been the default; I'm not sure how it ended up being an SSH url for you.

I swapped over to HTTPS, ran the cmd above and tried again but got the same result.

```
➜  desktop git:(development) brew install github/gh/gh
==> Installing gh from github/gh
==> Downloading https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh/releases/download/v6.6.6/gh_6.6.6_macOS_amd64.tar.gz
#=#=-#  #
curl: (22) The requested URL returned error: 404 Not Found
Error: An exception occurred within a child process:
  DownloadError: Failed to download resource "gh"
Download failed: https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh/releases/download/v6.6.6/gh_6.6.6_macOS_amd64.tar.gz
```

While `brew update` gave me:

```
➜  gh-cli git:(master) ✗ brew update
git@github.com: Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.

Please make sure you have the correct access rights
and the repository exists.
Error: Fetching /usr/local/Homebrew/Library/Taps/github/homebrew-gh failed!
```

This was the output of the HTTPS cmd, not sure it worked based on:

```
➜  gh-cli git:(master) ✗ git --git-dir=/usr/local/Homebrew/Library/Taps/github/homebrew-gh/.git remote set-url https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh.git
usage: git remote set-url [--push] <name> <newurl> [<oldurl>]
   or: git remote set-url --add <name> <newurl>
   or: git remote set-url --delete <name> <url>
    --push                manipulate push URLs
    --add                 add URL
    --delete              delete URLs
```

Ah sorry, I wrote you the wrong command to swap the remote url. This should do it:

```
git --git-dir=/usr/local/Homebrew/Library/Taps/github/homebrew-gh/.git remote set-url origin https://github.com/github/homebrew-gh.git
```
Great, thank you!!!
